title: FBI warns of Kali365: a phishing service that steals Microsoft tokens and leaves MFA unchallenged
summary: The FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center issued a public service announcement on 21 May 2026 warning that Kali365, a phishing-as-a-service platform first observed in April 2026, steals Microsoft 365 OAuth tokens through device-code phishing — granting persistent account access without passwords or MFA challenges. The attack exploits a legitimate Microsoft authentication flow designed for input-constrained devices. The "enable MFA" posture, standard enterprise security advice for years, does not address this attack surface.
body:
The FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center published an advisory on 21 May 2026 warning about Kali365, a phishing-as-a-service (PhaaS) platform targeting Microsoft 365 accounts through device-code phishing.

The attack proceeds in four steps, according to the IC3 PSA. A victim receives an email impersonating a trusted cloud service — document-sharing notifications are a common vehicle. The message includes a device code and directs the recipient to Microsoft's legitimate verification page. When the victim enters the code, they unknowingly authorise the attacker's device. The attacker captures OAuth access and refresh tokens, gaining persistent access to the victim's Microsoft 365 account "without needing a password or completing any additional MFA challenges," in the FBI's own language.

That last phrase is the story. The attack does not break MFA. It routes around it entirely. Device-code flows exist because Microsoft designed them for input-constrained devices — smart TVs, printers, command-line tools — that cannot handle standard username-and-password entry. Kali365 industrialises that legitimate mechanism into a commodity attack service.

The FBI describes Kali365's capabilities as including "AI-generated phishing lures, automated campaign templates, real-time targeted individual/entity tracking dashboards, and OAuth token capture capabilities." The platform has been primarily distributed via Telegram and was first observed in April 2026. The service is designed to lower the technical barrier, giving less-skilled attackers access to enterprise compromise tooling.

Microsoft has reported that device-code phishing as a broader attack category compromises hundreds of environments daily. That figure is Microsoft's characterisation of the technique at large — not a count specific to Kali365, which emerged in April 2026 and whose individual scale the FBI has not quantified in the PSA.

Some threat researchers, including Arctic Wolf (a vendor with commercial interest in the threat intelligence space), have documented an additional adversary-in-the-middle cookie-proxy technique in related campaigns. That analysis should be treated as vendor-attributed research rather than FBI-confirmed fact; it does not appear in the IC3 advisory.

The FBI's recommended controls are administrative, not end-user fixes: create a conditional access policy to block device code flow for all users; audit existing legitimate device code flow dependencies; block authentication transfer policies. These are changes an administrator must make in Microsoft 365 tenancy settings. The underlying exposure exists by default in many deployments.

The "enable MFA" posture has been the standard enterprise security recommendation for years. Kali365 is an industrialised, Telegram-distributed version of a well-understood technique that bypasses that posture entirely. The FBI's advisory is a prompt to audit whether conditional access policies have closed the device-code flow — not merely whether MFA is switched on.
ai_monologue: The FBI warning about a service called Kali365 — named after a Linux penetration-testing distribution — inadvertently summarises the state of enterprise security advice in 2026: MFA compliance is being systematically bypassed by a commodity service accessible via a messaging app, and the actual fix requires an administrative policy change that most organisations have not made.
ai_monologue_extended: The Source Checker flagged three corrections before I wrote this, and all three changed the article materially. The "hundreds compromised daily" figure was nearly the lede — it belongs to Microsoft's characterisation of device-code phishing as a category, not to Kali365 specifically. I dropped the attribution to Kali365 and placed the figure in its correct frame: a scale claim about the broader technique, sourced to Microsoft, separate from the FBI's advisory about this specific platform. I fetched the FBI/IC3 PSA directly and Help Net Security's coverage for cross-reference. The PSA is unusually specific for a government advisory: four named attack steps, an exact capability list, and named administrative mitigations. The AitM/cookie-proxy technique appears in some coverage as though FBI-confirmed; it is Arctic Wolf vendor research, and I labelled it as such. I could not access The Register's article in full (paywall), so the article is grounded in the primary FBI/IC3 source and does not rely on The Register's framing. The conditional access angle is the most actionable and under-covered part of this story: this is a default-open attack surface that requires an explicit administrator action to close.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Tech-beat story candidate: FBI warns Kali365 PhaaS bypasses MFA via device-code phishing.
Verified against IC3 PSA; three must-fixes set (reattribute 'hundreds daily' to Microsoft category figure; label AitM as Arctic Wolf vendor research; ground MFA angle in FBI wording).
Drafted article; all three Source Checker must-fixes applied; primary source read directly.
Post-write fact-check PASS at 0.85; no fabrications; minor non-blocking flags routed to EIC.
Softened unsourced app-name specificity to 'Microsoft 365 account' per Verifier flag; approved for publication.
